title: Listening - Part 4: Making Artisan Chocolates
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Sofia, who runs a small chocolate-making business. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: In the studio today is Sofia Mendes, who makes chocolates in her own small workshop. Sofia, how did that begin? || Sofia: I was a laboratory assistant in a food company, so I already understood ingredients, but I wasn’t making sweets. During a holiday in Belgium, I attended a one-day chocolate class simply for fun. [24]I was fascinated that tiny changes in temperature could completely alter the shine and texture of chocolate[/24]. When I returned home, I started practising in my kitchen every weekend. || Presenter: When did you decide to sell your chocolates? || Sofia: Friends were happy to eat my experiments, but that did not prove strangers would pay for them. I rented a table at a winter market with only four flavours. [25]I sold everything before lunchtime and customers returned asking whether I had more boxes available[/25]. That response persuaded me to develop the business rather than keep it as a hobby. || Presenter: What was difficult about moving into a workshop? || Sofia: I imagined the biggest challenge would be buying equipment. Actually, choosing machines was straightforward. [26]It took much longer to learn all the hygiene records and food-labelling rules required before I could open legally[/26]. They are essential, of course, but I had not realised how much paperwork a tiny food business needs. || Presenter: How do you choose new flavours? || Sofia: I avoid creating flavours just because they sound unusual. Some combinations make excellent photographs but are not pleasant to eat. [27]I test each idea with a small group of regular customers and keep it only if they want a second piece[/27]. Their honest reactions prevent me from producing boxes nobody actually enjoys. || Presenter: People say chocolate making is artistic. Do you agree? || Sofia: There is creativity, especially when decorating individual chocolates, but precision comes first. If chocolate is too warm or too cool, it will not set correctly. [28]The part I enjoy most is achieving exactly the right smooth finish through careful control of temperature[/28]. It still feels satisfying every time I open a mould and see perfect results. || Presenter: Has demand ever caused difficulties? || Sofia: Last December an online magazine mentioned my gift boxes, and orders doubled in a day. At first I was delighted, then realised I could not make every order on time. [29]I temporarily limited the number of boxes customers could buy so that quality would not suffer[/29]. It was better to disappoint a few people than send out rushed products. || Presenter: What is your advice to somebody hoping to start a similar business? || Sofia: Take a practical course and practise a lot, but also calculate costs accurately. [30]Ingredients, packaging and the time spent making each box must all be included before you decide what price to charge[/30]. Passion is important, but it does not pay bills by itself.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first attracted Sofia to making chocolate?

options.A: The scientific effect of temperature on the result.
options.B: The possibility of leaving her job immediately.
options.C: The decorative designs she saw in a shop.

questions.explanation: Sofia became fascinated by the effect small temperature changes had on chocolate.

questions.question: Why did Sofia decide to turn chocolate making into a business?

options.A: Friends offered to invest in a workshop.
options.B: Her products sold very successfully at a market.
options.C: A food company asked her to supply gifts.

questions.explanation: At a winter market she sold out early and customers asked for more.

questions.question: What surprised Sofia about preparing to open her workshop?

options.A: The difficulty of finding suitable equipment.
options.B: The amount of official food-business administration.
options.C: The cost of testing new ingredients.

questions.explanation: She found the hygiene records and labelling regulations took longer than expected.

questions.question: How does Sofia decide whether to offer a new flavour?

options.A: She asks regular customers to try it.
options.B: She checks whether it looks attractive online.
options.C: She compares it with products from Belgium.

questions.explanation: She keeps a flavour only when test customers genuinely want another piece.

questions.question: What does Sofia enjoy most about making chocolates?

options.C: Producing a perfect texture and appearance.

questions.explanation: She enjoys controlling the temperature to obtain the smooth finish.

questions.question: How did Sofia respond to a sudden rise in orders?

options.B: She reduced sales temporarily to maintain standards.
options.C: She stopped taking orders through the internet.

questions.explanation: She limited order numbers so the chocolates would not be rushed.

questions.question: What does Sofia advise new chocolate makers to do?

options.A: Set prices by considering every production cost.
options.B: Spend most of their money on packaging.
options.C: Offer low prices until they become well known.

questions.explanation: She says ingredients, packaging and working time all need to be included in pricing.
